claims.statement: The canonical upstream repository is github.com/generalui/s3p.
claims.qualification: The latest git release tag is v3.6.0 while the npm latest dist-tag is 3.7.2.

claims.statement: s3p is written in CaffeineScript compiled to JavaScript and runs on Node.js using the AWS SDK for JavaScript v3 client-s3.
claims.qualification: CaffeineScript is a CoffeeScript-family language distributed as prebuilt JavaScript in the npm tarball.

claims.statement: The license is ISC.
claims.qualification: The inherited page recorded MIT; LICENSE.md and package.json both declare ISC.

claims.statement: The smoked artifact is s3p 3.7.2, the npm latest dist-tag, while source anchors use git tag v3.6.0.
claims.qualification: The inherited page left the version unconfirmed. The capability receipts recorded the version as caller-supplied metadata, but the build note independently captured the tool's own s3p version self-report of 3.7.2.

claims.statement: Listing is a first-class isolable operation because ls and summarize are standalone, non-mutating, list-only subcommands.
claims.qualification: This reframes the inherited testability open question and corrects the inherited claim that listing might not be separately invokable. Install for the tagged version is not straightforward (claim v3-6-0-cannot-start) and timing is blocked by authentication (claim anonymous-listing-blocked-at-auth). The credential-starved ls probe reached and issued real listObjectsV2 calls before the auth failure.

claims.statement: s3p discovers an unknown keyspace by recursive bisection with synthetic midpoint keys rather than a continuation-token page loop.
claims.qualification: The recursive engine eachRecursive was re-pinned to S3Comprehensions.caf:361-503 during review.

claims.statement: s3p is the only key-space bisector among the surveyed tools.
claims.qualification: The mechanism is source-confirmed, but the comparative uniqueness is the author's framing; the other study tools' listing strategies were not audited this phase.

evidence.reason: The other study tools' listing strategies were not audited, so the comparative claim cannot be established from s3p's source alone.

claims.statement: getBisectKey selects a synthetic midpoint by walking to the first divergent character and choosing the middle alphabet character, without sampling any real keys.
claims.qualification: The selection has special cases for adjacent characters and for incrementing to the next key.

claims.statement: Each split node issues two concurrent ListObjectsV2 calls, one at startAfter and one at the computed midpoint, and recurses a side only when its page returned a full 1000 keys.
claims.qualification: A full 1000-key page means the range still holds more keys; a non-full page means that half is exhausted. The tool page frames this full-page-then-split heuristic as a claimed strength that is simple and requires no tuning; that positive framing is not receipt-settled.

claims.statement: The credential-starved ls probe scheduled two listObjectsV2 attempts with distinct StartAfter values before both aborted at credential resolution.
claims.qualification: This shows the fan-out logic executes; it does not show simultaneous on-the-wire execution because both attempts aborted before any HTTP LIST and the receipt carries no timestamps.

claims.statement: The two per-node LIST calls keep disjoint kept-sets, the left filtered to Key<=middleKey and the right to Key<=stopAt.
claims.qualification: The inherited fifty-percent kept-set overlap is corrected: any overlap is redundant raw fetching in dense ranges, not a fifty-fifty kept-set overlap.

claims.statement: The fraction of wasted LIST work is around fifty percent.
claims.qualification: The waste fraction is scale-dependent and was not measured; a benchmark would count logical LIST requests against unique keys returned. The tool page frames the eager double-LIST-per-node as a claimed strength that trades this extra LIST work for lower discovery latency with no full-scan prepass; that advantage is unverified.

evidence.reason: No instrumented request-count run was possible; listing was auth-blocked.

claims.statement: A LIFO PromiseWorkerPool caps simultaneous LIST requests at listConcurrency, which defaults to 100.
claims.qualification: The pool location was re-pinned to PromiseWorkerPool.caf:26-48 (push at :30, pop at :40) and the default to S3Comprehensions.caf:246. It is exposed as --list-concurrency.

claims.statement: The usePrefixBisect mode is library-internal and auto-triggered when a right page returns zero items, not a user-selectable CLI flag.
claims.qualification: The inherited page described it as opt-in; there is no CLI flag and it is absent from all --help output.

claims.statement: Prefix bisection does not improve performance.
claims.qualification: This is the author's own unreproduced finding; benchmarking it would require an API driver because there is no CLI flag.

evidence.reason: The author's no-faster finding was not independently reproduced.

claims.statement: The supported key alphabet is exactly 95 printable-ASCII characters, from space 0x20 through tilde 0x7E.
claims.qualification: The inherited page disputed 94 versus 96; the count is 0x7E minus 0x20 plus 1, which is 95.

claims.statement: The published throughput and speedup figures for s3p are all author self-reports with no third-party reproduction.
claims.qualification: The figures trace to distinct sources with distinct provenance strength: about 20,000 items per second is the GenUI accelerators page; about 35,000 items per second peak and the 5-to-50-times overall multiplier are the author's Medium post, which also gives a 9 GB/s copy rate; an independently read README figure of almost 50,000 items per second as of v3.5 and an 8-to-9 GB/s copy rate were found in the same author lineage; a separate secondhand summary table carries an unanchored about 35,000 objects per second with no bucket size or wall-clock; and the conflicting 15-to-100-times multiplier is a landscape survey. The 9 GB/s figure is a copy rate, not a listing rate; the two multipliers conflict; and both multipliers trace to the author.

claims.statement: The published throughput figures reproduce on the study setup.
claims.qualification: No third-party reproduction exists; smoke produces no comparative numbers and listing was auth-blocked.

evidence.reason: No benchmark or comparative run has been performed.

claims.statement: The requests and listRequests counters count logical s3.list operations, incremented by two per node before the call, not underlying HTTP attempts.
claims.qualification: SDK-level retries are invisible to the counter, so true wire cost must be measured by counting HTTP requests independently.

claims.statement: Blind ASCII-midpoint bisection costs extra rounds on skewed keyspaces.
claims.qualification: This is a benchmark-phase question, falsifiable by running a skewed layout against a uniform bucket of the same size; not run.

evidence.reason: No skewed-keyspace run has been performed.

claims.statement: s3p runs as a single-core Node.js process.
claims.qualification: The author states s3p is still only a single-core Node.js application; a single Node event loop drives up to listConcurrency concurrent SDK calls.

claims.statement: Throughput plateaus well below multi-core tools at high listConcurrency.
claims.qualification: The single-core architecture is corroborated, but the plateau is scale-dependent and not settleable at smoke scale.

evidence.reason: No concurrency sweep or plateau measurement has been performed.

claims.statement: The CLI ls path streams each item as it arrives and releases page arrays as they are consumed.
claims.qualification: This is a source-structure observation about the CLI path holding no in-memory key buffer; it does not show the CLI cannot run out of memory at scale.

claims.statement: The library ls and list API accumulates all keys into one array.
claims.qualification: This is a memory footgun at scale for API users but does not apply to the streaming CLI path.

claims.statement: s3p runs out of memory at around 100 million objects as reported in issue 23.
claims.qualification: Issue 23 was not read this phase and the reported OOM is unattributed; the source establishes only that the library API accumulates, not that it causes the reported failure. That scale is unreachable while listing is auth-blocked.

evidence.reason: The reported OOM scale could not be reached and issue 23 was not investigated.

claims.statement: s3p adds no retry of its own but does not disable the AWS SDK v3 client's default retries.
claims.qualification: The inherited no-retry weakness is corrected: list only wraps the call with a tapCatch that logs and re-throws, and the S3Client is built with no retry override, so retries are not absent.

claims.statement: A transient 503 or SlowDown response during a run crashes or corrupts the run rather than being absorbed.
claims.qualification: This is the inherited weakness hypothesis, carried in full. Its no-retry premise is corrected by source because the AWS SDK v3 default retries remain (claim no-s3p-level-retry-sdk-defaults-remain), but whether the run survives sustained throttling is unrun and falsifiable by inducing throttling and observing whether the run survives.

evidence.reason: No throttling or fault-injection run has been performed.

claims.statement: A key containing any character outside the 95-character alphabet makes getKeyCharIndex return -1 and getBisectKey throw "Invalid character found in inputs".
claims.qualification: This source correction contradicts the earlier secondhand claim that s3p works on any keyspace including non-printable bytes. The throw site was re-pinned to S3Keys.caf:57-58.

claims.statement: Bisection actually throws or mis-partitions keys on a real bucket containing non-ASCII keys.
claims.qualification: EDGE_BUCKET was not configured, so the character-set boundary was not exercised against a live listing.

evidence.reason: No edge-case bucket was configured or listed; listing was auth-blocked.

claims.statement: Native UTF-16 comparisons run before the alphabet-lookup throw, so a BMP-then-astral boundary pair can mis-decide a range empty and return before the throw.
claims.qualification: This corrects the earlier read that the UTF-16 divergence was subsumed by the non-ASCII throw: getBisectKey opens with if startAfter < stopAt and eachRecursive guards on startAfter >= stopAt, both before the charIndex-below-zero throw.

claims.statement: A real astral-character boundary pair produces an observable mis-ordering or early return during a listing.
claims.qualification: The observable effect was not run; EDGE_BUCKET was not configured.

evidence.reason: No astral-character edge bucket was configured or listed.

claims.statement: The author's self-flagged bisectKey-after-stopAt bug is guarded by a runtime post-condition assertion that throws "Whoops!" unless startAfter <= bisectKey <= stopAt.
claims.qualification: The verbatim author comment is at S3Keys.caf:36 and the guarding assertion at S3Keys.caf:87-88.

claims.statement: The guarded bisectKey post-condition produces an observable failure once the bisection path is exercised.
claims.qualification: What observable failure, if any, the assertion produces was not run.

evidence.reason: The bisection path was not exercised against a live listing.

claims.statement: s3p has no anonymous or unsigned request path: the S3Client is built with region and endpoint only, and no --no-sign-request or credentials hook exists in the source or CLI.
claims.qualification: The absence was confirmed in the v3.6.0 source and against the live 3.7.2 help.

claims.statement: Under credential-starved anonymous mode the ls, ls --raw, and summarize probes all failed at AWS-SDK credential resolution with exit 1 before any LIST completed.
claims.qualification: The block is command-independent across two subcommands; ls --long shares the same code path and was marked blocked-by-inheritance rather than re-run. Scoped to s3p 3.7.2, anonymous mode, and the registered smoke bucket.

claims.statement: Git tags and GitHub releases lag npm: the latest tag is v3.6.0, master HEAD is 16 untagged commits ahead at version 3.6.1, and npm latest 3.7.2 has no corresponding git commit.
claims.qualification: A reader trusting GitHub releases would pin a version that cannot start.

claims.statement: The published v3.6.0 npm artifact cannot start: s3p version throws Cannot find module 'colors' because colors is required at runtime but absent from the published dependency closure; it was fixed in 3.6.1.
claims.qualification: This was observed via a direct docker run of an s3p@3.6.0 image documented in a build note, not a wrapper smoke receipt, so it does not carry confirmed run status.

claims.statement: s3p 3.7.x adds --max-sockets, --endpoint and S3_ENDPOINT, --group-by, and a mutating delete command, none present in the v3.6.0 source.
claims.qualification: The --max-sockets, --group-by, and delete additions were observed from the live help captured in the build note; the --endpoint and S3_ENDPOINT override is not in that build note and is recorded in the research report from the 3.7.2 build. None is a wrapper smoke receipt.

claims.statement: The --max-list-requests option is a soft cap: it is checked before each increment of two, so it can overshoot the budget by up to one pair.
claims.qualification: On hitting the budget the run aborts nicely, finishing in-flight calls, logging, and throwing with stats.

claims.statement: The ls --long mode emits a human-rounded size in a space-joined line, so it is lossy and not a verification mode.
claims.qualification: A key is recoverable from this format only when it contains no spaces; ls --raw is the exact mode.

claims.statement: The normalize.sh mode contracts are source-derived and were validated only against synthetic adapter fixtures because live listing was auth-blocked.
claims.qualification: The fixtures under adapter/fixtures cover ls-raw, ls, ls-long, summarize, and empty input; no live tool output could be captured.
